questions.question: An Ingress has two rules: host 'api.example.com' routes to the 'api-svc' Service, and host 'web.example.com' routes to the 'web-svc' Service. What is this routing pattern called?
options: Path-based routing
Name-based virtual hosting
Load-balanced routing
Round-robin routing
questions.explanation: Name-based virtual hosting routes traffic based on the Host header in HTTP requests. The same IP address serves multiple domain names, with the Ingress controller directing traffic to different backend Services based on which hostname the client requested.

questions.question: An Ingress resource has path type 'Prefix' with path '/api'. Which requests match this rule?
options: Only exactly '/api'
'/api', '/api/', '/api/users', '/api/v2/items', and any path starting with '/api'
'/api' and '/apikeys'
Only '/api/' with a trailing slash
questions.explanation: With pathType: Prefix, the Ingress matches any path that starts with the specified prefix when split on '/'. '/api' matches '/api', '/api/', and '/api/users'. It does NOT match '/apikeys' because prefix matching is done on path segments (split by '/'), not arbitrary string prefixes.

questions.question: What is the purpose of a NetworkPolicy in Kubernetes?
options: To configure DNS resolution for Services
To control which Pods can communicate with each other and with external endpoints
To set bandwidth limits on Pod traffic
To manage TLS certificates for Services
questions.explanation: NetworkPolicies control ingress (incoming) and egress (outgoing) traffic to and from Pods based on label selectors, namespace selectors, and IP blocks. By default, all Pods can communicate with all other Pods. NetworkPolicies restrict this to only allowed traffic patterns.

questions.question: A NetworkPolicy selects Pods with label 'app: web' and allows ingress only from Pods with label 'app: api'. What happens to traffic from a Pod labeled 'app: monitoring' to the web Pods?
options: The traffic is allowed because monitoring Pods are trusted by default
The traffic is denied because only Pods with label 'app: api' are allowed in the ingress rule
The traffic is allowed but rate-limited
The traffic is logged but not blocked
questions.explanation: Once a NetworkPolicy selects a Pod, all ingress (or egress) traffic to that Pod is denied by default unless explicitly allowed by a rule. Since only 'app: api' Pods are allowed in the ingress rules, traffic from 'app: monitoring' Pods is blocked. This is a deny-by-default model.

questions.question: A NetworkPolicy has both ingress and egress rules defined. Another NetworkPolicy in the same namespace selects the same Pods but with different rules. How do they interact?
options: The more restrictive policy wins
The less restrictive policy wins
NetworkPolicies are additive -- the union of all rules applies
The most recently created policy replaces the older one
questions.explanation: NetworkPolicies are additive (union-based). If multiple policies select the same Pod, the allowed traffic is the union of all their rules. No policy can deny what another allows. This means adding a NetworkPolicy can only make access more permissive (or restrict traffic on a dimension not yet covered), never more restrictive than the combination.

questions.question: A Service has 'spec.sessionAffinity: ClientIP'. What effect does this have?
options: All traffic goes to a single Pod regardless of source
Requests from the same client IP are routed to the same Pod for the duration of the session
The Service only accepts connections from whitelisted IP addresses
The Service maintains TCP connections to all Pods simultaneously
questions.explanation: SessionAffinity: ClientIP configures the Service to route requests from the same client IP address to the same backend Pod. This is sometimes called 'sticky sessions'. The timeout can be configured with sessionAffinityConfig.clientIP.timeoutSeconds (default 10800 = 3 hours).

questions.question: How do you configure TLS termination on an Ingress resource?
options: Add a TLS certificate directly in the Ingress annotations
Reference a Kubernetes TLS Secret in the Ingress spec's tls section
Configure TLS on each backend Service individually
Install a TLS sidecar in every Pod behind the Ingress
questions.explanation: TLS termination on Ingress is configured by referencing a kubernetes.io/tls Secret in the Ingress spec's 'tls' section. The Secret contains the TLS certificate and private key. The Ingress controller terminates TLS and forwards decrypted HTTP traffic to backend Services.

questions.question: A headless Service (clusterIP: None) is created for a StatefulSet with 3 replicas named 'db'. What DNS records are created?
options: Only a single A record for the Service name
Individual A records for each Pod: db-0.svc-name.namespace.svc.cluster.local, db-1.svc-name.namespace.svc.cluster.local, etc.
CNAME records pointing to an external DNS provider
No DNS records are created for headless Services
questions.explanation: A headless Service for a StatefulSet creates individual DNS A records for each Pod, following the pattern <pod-name>.<service-name>.<namespace>.svc.cluster.local. This enables direct Pod-to-Pod communication by stable DNS names (db-0.svc-name, db-1.svc-name, etc.), which is essential for stateful applications like databases.

questions.question: By default, without any NetworkPolicies, what is the network behavior for Pods in a namespace?
options: All ingress traffic is denied
All egress traffic is denied
All ingress and egress traffic is allowed
Only traffic within the same namespace is allowed
questions.explanation: Without any NetworkPolicies, Kubernetes allows all ingress and egress traffic to all Pods. NetworkPolicies are opt-in -- once you create a policy selecting a Pod, that Pod's traffic is restricted to only what the policy allows. Pods not selected by any policy continue to allow all traffic.

questions.question: A Service selects Pods using the label 'app: backend'. What happens when no Pods match this selector?
options: The Service automatically creates Pods with the matching label
The Service has an empty Endpoints list and connections to it fail
The Service routes traffic to Pods in other namespaces with the same label
The Service is automatically deleted after a timeout
questions.explanation: When no Pods match a Service's selector, the associated Endpoints resource has no entries. Any attempt to connect to the Service will fail because there are no backend Pods to receive traffic. The Service itself remains active and will start routing once matching Pods appear.

questions.question: An Ingress has a defaultBackend configured and a rule for path '/api' routing to 'api-svc'. A request arrives for path '/dashboard'. Where is it routed?
options: The request is rejected with a 404 error
The request is routed to the defaultBackend Service
The request is routed to 'api-svc' because it is the only rule
The Ingress controller returns a 503 Service Unavailable
questions.explanation: The defaultBackend handles requests that do not match any Ingress rules. Since '/dashboard' does not match the '/api' rule, it falls through to the defaultBackend. Without a defaultBackend, the Ingress controller typically returns a 404 for unmatched paths.

questions.question: A NetworkPolicy allows egress traffic to CIDR block 10.0.0.0/8 on port 5432. What type of traffic does this permit?
options: Incoming database connections from the 10.0.0.0/8 network
Outgoing connections to any IP in the 10.0.0.0/8 range on port 5432
Both incoming and outgoing traffic on port 5432
DNS resolution for addresses in the 10.0.0.0/8 range
questions.explanation: An egress rule with CIDR 10.0.0.0/8 and port 5432 allows outgoing TCP connections from the selected Pods to any IP address in the 10.0.0.0/8 range on port 5432. This is a typical pattern for allowing database connections to internal services while blocking other outbound traffic.

questions.question: A Pod in the namespace 'dev' wants to connect to a Service named 'redis' in the same namespace. Which DNS name can it use?
options: redis.dev.svc.cluster.local only
redis.svc.cluster.local
redis (the short name works within the same namespace)
cluster.local.svc.dev.redis
questions.explanation: Within the same namespace, Pods can use the short Service name ('redis') for DNS resolution. The cluster DNS automatically appends the search domain (dev.svc.cluster.local) to resolve the full name. The FQDN 'redis.dev.svc.cluster.local' also works but is unnecessary within the same namespace.

questions.question: A Service has 'spec.internalTrafficPolicy: Local'. What effect does this have?
options: The Service only accepts traffic from within the cluster
Traffic is routed only to endpoints on the same node as the client Pod, avoiding cross-node hops
The Service uses local storage for session state
Traffic is encrypted within the cluster
questions.explanation: internalTrafficPolicy: Local restricts internal traffic to endpoints on the same node as the requesting Pod. This reduces latency and cross-node traffic. If no local endpoints exist on the node, the traffic is dropped. This is similar to externalTrafficPolicy: Local but for internal cluster traffic.

questions.question: What is an IngressClass used for?
options: To classify traffic as HTTP or HTTPS
To specify which Ingress controller should handle a particular Ingress resource
To define network policies for Ingress traffic
To set QoS classes for Ingress-routed Pods
questions.explanation: IngressClass specifies which Ingress controller should implement a given Ingress resource. When multiple Ingress controllers are installed (e.g., NGINX and Traefik), the IngressClass lets you choose which one handles each Ingress. It is set via the ingressClassName field in the Ingress spec.

questions.question: You want to create a 'deny all ingress' default NetworkPolicy for a namespace. Which spec achieves this?
options: podSelector: {}, policyTypes: ['Ingress'] with no ingress rules defined
podSelector: {matchLabels: {deny: all}}, policyTypes: ['Ingress']
podSelector: {}, policyTypes: ['Ingress', 'Egress'] with ingress: [{}]
podSelector: {}, policyTypes: ['Ingress'] with ingress: [{from: [{podSelector: {}}]}]
questions.explanation: An empty podSelector ({}) selects all Pods in the namespace. policyTypes: ['Ingress'] with no ingress rules means no ingress traffic is allowed. This creates a default deny-all policy for incoming traffic. Any subsequent NetworkPolicies can then selectively allow specific traffic patterns.

questions.question: What is the port range for NodePort Services by default?
questions.explanation: By default, Kubernetes allocates NodePort Services a port in the range 30000-32767. This range can be configured with the --service-node-port-range flag on the API server. You can also specify a particular port within this range in the Service spec.

questions.question: An Ingress rule has pathType 'Exact' with path '/api/v1/users'. Which requests does it match?
options: /api/v1/users and /api/v1/users/
Only exactly /api/v1/users (no trailing slash, no sub-paths)
/api/v1/users, /api/v1/users/123, and /api/v1/users?page=1
Any path containing 'users'
questions.explanation: pathType: Exact matches only the exact path specified. '/api/v1/users' matches only that exact path -- not '/api/v1/users/' (with trailing slash), not '/api/v1/users/123' (with sub-path). Query parameters are not part of path matching, so '/api/v1/users?page=1' would match the path component.

questions.question: What DNS record type does Kubernetes create for a ClusterIP Service?
options: CNAME record pointing to the cluster DNS
A record mapping the Service name to the ClusterIP address
SRV record with port information only
TXT record with Service metadata
questions.explanation: For a ClusterIP Service, Kubernetes DNS creates an A record (for IPv4) that maps the Service name to its ClusterIP address. SRV records are also created for named ports. This allows Pods to discover Services using standard DNS resolution.

questions.question: A NetworkPolicy allows egress to a namespaceSelector matching label 'env: production'. What does this permit?
options: Outgoing traffic to any Pod in namespaces labeled 'env: production'
Incoming traffic from namespaces labeled 'env: production'
Both ingress and egress traffic with production namespaces
Outgoing traffic only to Services (not Pods) in production namespaces
questions.explanation: An egress rule with namespaceSelector allows outgoing traffic from the selected Pods to any Pod in namespaces matching the label selector. In this case, the Pods can send traffic to any Pod in any namespace labeled 'env: production'. This is a namespace-level egress control.

questions.question: Which field in a Service spec determines which Pods receive traffic from the Service?
options: targetPort
nodePort
selector
port
questions.explanation: The selector field in a Service spec uses label matching to determine which Pods are endpoints for the Service. Pods whose labels match the Service's selector receive traffic. The port field defines the Service's exposed port, and targetPort specifies the port on the Pod containers.

questions.question: A Service spec has 'port: 80' and 'targetPort: 8080'. A Pod container listens on port 8080. How does the traffic flow?
options: Clients connect to port 8080, Service forwards to port 80
Clients connect to port 80 on the Service, which forwards to port 8080 on the Pod
Both ports 80 and 8080 are exposed externally
The Service listens on both ports simultaneously
questions.explanation: The Service 'port' (80) is what clients connect to. The 'targetPort' (8080) is the port on the actual Pod container where traffic is forwarded. This decouples the Service's exposed port from the application's listening port, allowing flexible port mapping.

questions.question: An Ingress has a rewrite annotation that changes the path from '/app(/|$)(.*)' to '/$2'. A request for '/app/dashboard/settings' arrives. What path does the backend Service receive?
options: /app/dashboard/settings
/dashboard/settings
/app
questions.explanation: The regex captures '/app' and everything after it. The capture group $2 contains 'dashboard/settings'. The rewrite target '/$2' sends '/dashboard/settings' to the backend. This is a common pattern for stripping path prefixes when routing to backend Services that expect paths relative to their own root.
